coredata.dc:title: Bifunctional effects of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots on CoS2/mesoporous carbon composites for high-performance lithium-ion batteries
coredata.dc:creator: Lee, Hae Ri
Kim, Yun Sik
Lee, Seon Yeong
Son, U Hyeok
Lee, Sungho
Joh, Han-Ik
coredata.dc:description: Cobalt disulfide (CoS2) stands as a promising candidate for anode materials in lithium-ion batteries due to its high theoretical capacity, but it faces challenges associated with the shuttle effect of lithium polysulfide during cycling. To address these issues, zeolitic imidazolate framework (ZIF)-derived composites have been extensively explored because of distinct advantages such as the formation of nano-sized particles, heteroatom doping, and highly porous structures. However, ZIF-derived carbon supports primarily consist of ultra-micropores that can impede lithium-ion diffusion. Herein, we aimed to enhance cycling stability by introducing a nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot (NCQD) solution derived from N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone into cobalt-based ZIF-67 to modify the porosity and dope heteroatoms of CoS2 nanoparticle-embedded heteroatom-doped carbon composites (CoS2/NSC). The mildly acidic NCQD solution resulted in the partial etching of the ZIF-67 structure, along with the deposition of NCQDs as a nitrogen source. Notably, the pore sizes could be adjusted by varying the concentration of the NCQD solution, while retaining the nitrogen functional groups during carbonization. The electrode using CoS2/NSC with the 2.8 mL NCQD pre-treatment exhibited enhanced C-rate capability with the capacity of 392 mAh/g at 2.0 A/g. Moreover, the cycling stability was improved, with a capacity retention of 77 % after 100 cycles.

(c) Capacitive contribution of CoS2/NSC-2.8 at 0.2 mV s−1 and (d) contribution ratio of capacitive- and diffusion controlled for CoS2/NSC-0 and CoS2/NSC-2.8 in various scan rates.

head.ce:title: Bifunctional effects of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots on CoS2/mesoporous carbon composites for high-performance lithium-ion batteries

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this manuscript.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cobalt disulfide (CoS2) stands as a promising candidate for anode materials in lithium-ion batteries due to its high theoretical capacity, but it faces challenges associated with the shuttle effect of lithium polysulfide during cycling. To address these issues, zeolitic imidazolate framework (ZIF)-derived composites have been extensively explored because of distinct advantages such as the formation of nano-sized particles, heteroatom doping, and highly porous structures. However, ZIF-derived carbon supports primarily consist of ultra-micropores that can impede lithium-ion diffusion. Herein, we aimed to enhance cycling stability by introducing a nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot (NCQD) solution derived from N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone into cobalt-based ZIF-67 to modify the porosity and dope heteroatoms of CoS2 nanoparticle-embedded heteroatom-doped carbon composites (CoS2/NSC). The mildly acidic NCQD solution resulted in the partial etching of the ZIF-67 structure, along with the deposition of NCQDs as a nitrogen source. Notably, the pore sizes could be adjusted by varying the concentration of the NCQD solution, while retaining the nitrogen functional groups during carbonization. The electrode using CoS2/NSC with the 2.8 mL NCQD pre-treatment exhibited enhanced C-rate capability with the capacity of 392 mAh/g at 2.0 A/g. Moreover, the cycling stability was improved, with a capacity retention of 77 % after 100 cycles.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have become an essential power source for portable electronic devices and electric vehicles owing to their high energy densities and long cycle lives [1,2]. The performance of LIBs can be significantly enhanced by developing advanced electrode materials that offer greater capacity and improved stability compared to conventional graphite, which has a low energy density of approximately 372 mAh/g [3]. Transition metal sulfides (TMSs) have been explored as promising electrode materials for LIBs because of their high theoretical capacity, which typically originates from conversion reactions, and good electrochemical activity [4]. Cobalt disulfide (CoS2) has been studied as an anode material because of its high theoretical capacity (872 mAh/g), relatively high electronic conductivity compared to other TMSs, and thermal stability [5]. However, they have serious disadvantages like other TMSs such as large volume changes and lithium polysulfide shuttle effects, which result in the loss of active materials during cycling [6–8]. Several strategies have been proposed to resolve the challenges in LIB technology. These strategies include using nano-sized CoS2 particles, increasing porosity, and forming composite materials with carbonaceous supports. These strategies not only offer superior electronic conductivity but also help to reduce the volume changes during lithiation and delithiation. [9–12].

Recent studies on CoS2-based anodes have focused on the utilization of carbon supports with heteroatom doping, such as nitrogen, sulfur, and phosphorus, to enhance cycling stability [8,13–18]. It is well known that sulfur and nitrogen functional groups in carbon networks have high electronegativity, attracting lithium polysulfides and capturing them as well as providing active sites for alkaline ions including lithium ions [14–19]. In addition, the electron-rich pyridinic N groups not only have considerable thermodynamic stability but also show heightened Lewis-basicity in contrast to pyrrolic N, indicating the capability of strong dipole − dipole interaction with lithium polysulfides to form SxLi–N complexes. Simultaneously, sulfur doping within the carbon network allows strong attraction of polysulfide anions [20–23]. Thus, this approach promoted stable cycling through the composite formation of CoS2 nanoparticles on a heteroatom-doped carbon support.

One straightforward method to prepare CoS2/carbon composites is to utilize Co-containing metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) as precursors [24,25]. MOFs, characterized as porous materials comprising metal ions and coordinating organic ligands, have been applied in various fields such as energy storage materials, gas adsorbents, sensors, drug delivery, and electrochemical catalysts because of their highly ordered pore structures and large surface area [26–31]. This simple synthetic procedure requires no additional Co or N precursors and allows the formation of nano-sized CoS2 particles embedded in a nitrogen-doped carbon matrix (NC). Wang et al. initially synthesized CoS2/NC from zeolitic imidazolate framework (ZIF)-67 using a simple two-step carbonization process followed by sulfur vulcanization [8]. During carbonization at 650 ˚C, cobalt ions reduced to form Co nanoparticles (<15 nm), which are highly reactive to sulfur to form CoS2 during following sulfurization, whereas the coordinating organic ligands were decomposed into porous N-doped carbon support. The CoS2/NC anode material showed a capacity of 560 mAh/g after 50 cycles at a current density of 100 mA g−1 and good C-rate capability (410 mAh/g at 2500 mA g−1). Moreover, the authors observed severe capacity decay of CoS2/NC carbonized at a high temperature of 1,000 ˚C, despite its superior electronic conductivity. This outcome was attributed to the lower nitrogen content in comparison to CoS2/NC carbonized at 650 ˚C. Therefore, the authors insisted on the dominant roles of the nitrogen content and particle size of CoS2 in the electrochemical performance.

The porous structure of the active material is a pivotal factor influencing the electrochemical performance during the fast charge and discharge processes. Numerous studies have focused on the enlargement of pore sizes within active materials to establish pathways for Li-ion migration in the electrolyte [32–34]. Developing hierarchical pore structures with micropores and mesopores is particularly significant in active materials. Mesopores facilitate the diffusion of solvated Li ions through the electrolyte, whereas micropores provide a large surface area for Li-ion storage after desolvation [35]. Unfortunately, ZIF-67 mainly exhibits sub-micropores, which can induce the sluggish diffusion of Li ions and low lithiation/delithiation. Furthermore, the carbonization of ZIF-67 could lead to the shrinkage of the particle and a reduction in the surface area, because the micropores can readily collapse at high temperatures (above 900 ˚C) due to the decomposition of the imidazole ligands of ZIF-67 [36]. Thus, to enhance the performance of CoS2/NC derived from ZIF-67, an increase in the heteroatom content and the formation of a hierarchical porous structure are required, even after heat treatment.

In this study, we introduce a strategy to simultaneously control the porosity and heteroatom content of CoS2/NC during heat treatment through the straightforward pre-treatment of ZIF-67. We applied nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) derived from N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) to a ZIF-67 surface. The NCQDs were obtained by the decomposition of NMP under harsh oxidative conditions and used as a solution with NCQDs dispersed in NMP, which facilitated mixing with ZIF-67. Interestingly, the NCQD solution can act as an etchant as well as a nitrogen source because the oxidation of NMP can form derivatives containing nitrogen, resulting in a decrease in pH, which ultimately makes the solution acidic [37]. The acidification of ZIF-67 led to an enlargement of the pore size, followed by the coating of nitrogen-enriched carbon dots. The impact of the NCQD process on the formation rate, porosity, chemical configuration, and electrochemical performance of CoS2 nanoparticles was investigated.

Cobalt nitrate hexahydrate (Co(NO3)2·6H2O, 98 %) and thiourea (CH4N2S ≥ 99 %) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. 2-methy-limidazole (C4H6N2, 98 %), methanol (CH4O, ≥ 99.5 %), ethanol (C2H6O, ≥ 99.5 %), and N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (C5H9NO, 99.7 %) were obtained from Daejung Chem. All chemicals and reagents were used as received without further purification. Ultrapure water (>18.25 MΩ cm) was used for washing.

NCQDs solution was prepared by a simple heat treatment under temperature control, as reported previously [38]. NMP (70.0 ml) was heated at 170 ℃ for 10 h in a round-bottom flask connected with a reflux condenser and persistent O2 gas injection. Finally, a dark-brown viscous NCQDs solution was obtained.

3.0 g Co(NO3)2·6H2O and 6.0 g 2-methyl imidazole were dissolved in 200 and 400 ml methanol, respectively, under 1 h ultrasonication. The former was poured into the latter and stirred for 24 h at room temperature. The purple precipitates were centrifuged, washed with methanol and ethanol 3 times, and vacuum dried at 80 ˚C

The NCQDs coating was applied using a rotary evaporator. First, 20 mL of the NMP solvent was mixed with a controlled volume of the NCQDs solution in a vial. Then, 0.2 g of ZIF-67 powder was placed in a round-bottomed flask, and the diluted NCQDs solution was poured into the flask and sonicated for 1 h to distribute ZIF-67 homogeneously. Afterward, the flask was connected to a rotary evaporator and vacuum-dried at 150 ˚C for 3 h with rotation. The obtained dark purple powder is denoted as ZIF-67@NCQDs-x (x = 0, 0.7, 2.8, 7.0), where x represents the loaded volume (ml) of the NCQDs solution based on 1.0 g of ZIF-67.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Co/NMC-x and CoS2/NSC-x composite
ce:section.ce:para: ZIF-67@NCQDs-x powder was carbonized in the electrical furnace at 800 °C for 1 h under N2 atmosphere with a heating rate of 5 °C min−1. The pyrolyzed black powder was denoted as Co/NMC-x. Sulfidation was then performed using a solvothermal method with thiourea as the source of sulfur and nitrogen. Briefly, 80 mg Co/NMC-x powder was dispersed in thiourea-ethanol solution (15 mg ml−1), and the solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave, followed by heat treatment at 180 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the resulting product was washed thrice with ethanol and DI water. Finally, a black powder was obtained after vacuum drying at 80 °C for 12 h and denoted as CoS2/NSC-x.

The chemical structure, chemical etching, and coating effects of the NCQDs solution on ZIF-67 were evaluated using Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Perkin Elmer) and inductively coupled plasma mass spectroscopy (ICP-MS) (iCAP RQ, Thermo Fisher Scientific). The ZIF-67 powders, after the reaction with the NCQDs solution, were separated by filtration, and only the filtrates were analyzed. The in situ time-dependent pH variation of the diluted NCQDs solutions was also analyzed using a pH electrode (ST-230, OHAUS) at 0, 5, 30, 60, and 120 min after dipping the pristine ZIF-67 powders.

The morphologies and particle sizes of the samples were examined by field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, SU8010, Hitachi) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-1010, JEOL). Scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM, JEM-F200, JEOL) and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) were used to analyze the crystalline phase and elemental distribution of CoS2/NMC-x. X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku, SmartLab) analysis was performed with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15406 nm) in the range of 5–60° for ZIF-67@NCQDs-x and 20-70° for carbonized and sulfurized samples. N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms were measured using an ASAP-2460 (Micromeritics) at 77 K. The samples were degassed under a vacuum at 150 °C for 12 h. Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET), Horvath–Kawazoe (HK), and Barrett–Joyner–Halenda methods based on density functional theory calculations were used to obtain the specific surface areas and pore volumes. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA, TGA N-1000, Scinco) was performed with a temperature ramp of 10 °C min−1 to understand the composition of CoS2 in sulfurized samples. All samples were analyzed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, K-alpha, Thermo Scientific) to compare their crystal bonding configurations and oxidation states.

CoS2/NSC-x was tested as an anode material in half-cells of CR 2032-type coin cells. Slurries of the electrodes were prepared by mixing the active material, carbon black (Super P), and polyvinylidene fluoride in a weight ratio of 8:1:1 in NMP solvent, followed by coating on copper foil. Then, the electrodes were dried in a vacuum oven at 80 °C overnight. The diameter of the electrode was 15 mm and the average loading of the active material was 1.2 mg cm−2. Coin cells were assembled in an argon-filled glove box with Li metal foil, Celgard 2400 polypropylene, and 1 M LiPF6 dissolved in ethylene carbonate and diethyl carbonate (1:1 v/v). Galvanostatic charge/discharge and cyclic voltammetry (CV) tests were conducted using an automatic battery test system (WBCS 3000 L, Won A Tech). Galvanostatic charge/discharge was tested with current densities from 0.1 to 2.0 A g−1, whereas CV was performed in 0.2 mVs−1. All cycling tests were performed in the voltage between 0.01–3.0 V (vs Li/Li+). The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was conducted to measure the resistance by an electrochemical workstation (PGSTAT302N, Metrohm Autolab) in the frequency range of 0.01 to 1000 Hz with the 5 mV AC amplitude.

NCQDs were prepared by ring-opening reaction of NMP under mild oxidation conditions with O2 purging at 170 ˚C. The resultant NCQDs were obtained in solution form, in which nanosized NCQDs were dispersed within an unreacted NMP solution. Notably, the as-prepared NCQD solution was black (inset of Fig. S1(a)) and weakly acidic with a pH of 3.38. These observations are consistent with previous reports that NMP oxidation can occur during mild heat treatment in the presence of oxygen, yielding nitrogen-containing derivatives with diverse structures [37–40]. Interestingly, the structures of NMP derivatives have been proposed to be hexagonal-based units with oxygen species, such as N-methylsuccinimide (NMS) and 5-hydroxy-N-methylpyrrolidone (5-HNMP). Moreover, the pH of the NMP solution with NCQDs decreases proportionally with an increase in the content of derivatives containing exchangeable protons [37]. In the FT-IR spectra of the as-prepared NCQD solution (Fig. S1(b)), a broad peak at 3530 cm−1 appeared, corresponding to the hydroxyl groups, which confirmed the formation of the NMP derivatives. Furthermore, the prolonged oxidation of NMP promotes oxidative polymerization followed by carbonization, ultimately leading to the formation of nitrogen-rich carbon quantum dots [41]. The elevated temperatures and longer reaction times used in our NCQD solution synthesis yielded carbon dots with sizes ranging from 30 to 40 nm, as shown in the TEM images of the as-prepared NCQD solution (Fig. S1(a)). In the XPS survey data, it can be confirmed that the NCQDs have predominantly exhibited pyrrolic N with a high N content of 12.37 %.

An acidic NCQD solution was utilized to chemically etch and simultaneously coat N-rich carbon dots onto ZIF-67, a Co-based MOF, to form a hierarchical porous CoS2/NSC-x composite. The synthesis procedure is illustrated in Scheme 1 . ZIF-67 was mixed with a designated amount of the NCQD solution, followed by carbonization and sulfurization. The morphological changes in ZIF-67 exposed to the influence of the NCQDs were systematically investigated using FE-SEM and TEM, as depicted in Fig. 1 . Pristine ZIF-67 has a rhombic dodecahedral morphology, with an average size of 472 nm, as ascertained from the SEM images (Fig. 1 (a)). As the coated amount of the NCQD solution increased, the average particle size gradually increased in the range of 480–500 nm and the ZIF-67 particles tended to stick to each other, indicating that the NCQDs not only covered the surface of the ZIF-67 particles but also acted as a binder. Furthermore, in Fig. 1 (e)–(h), the TEM images of the NCQD-coated ZIF-67 particles show internal pores that were absent in the pristine ZIF-67 particles. ZIF-67@NCQDs-0.7 exhibited pores predominantly located close to the edges, whereas the pores were homogeneously distributed throughout the ZIF-67@NCQDs-2.8 particle domain. This confirmed that the acidic NCQD solution on the surface of ZIF-67 gradually decomposed the crystalline structure from the internal edges into the center, maintaining the dodecahedral structure. However, mixing with 7.0 mL of the NCQD solution resulted in significant enlargement of the pore sizes and even caused some particles to lose their unique morphologies.

The etching effect of the NCQD solution was further studied from the perspective of chemical changes using operando pH measurements and ICP-MS. The diluted NCQD solution had a pH of 5.83, indicating its weakly acidic nature. As shown in Figure S2, the introduction of ZIF-67 powder into the diluted NCQD solution led to a significant pH increase to 7.58 within 5 min. Subsequently, the change in pH with prolonged mixing time became negligible. This phenomenon can be attributed to the consumption of exchangeable protons from the NMP derivatives during the partial etching of ZIF-67. Consistent with a previous study on void engineering within a MOF by Hu et al., the pH gradually increased to 8 as a consequence of the reaction between the MOF and organic acid, which ultimately resulted in the formation of hollow MOF particles [42]. Furthermore, the protons in the surface-bound MOF molecules may penetrate the particles, leading to cleavage of the Co–N coordination bonds. Consequently, increased acidity can accelerate MOF decomposition. Considering the proposed mechanism, we expected that the weak acidity of our diluted NCQD solution would facilitate the formation of hollow MOFs while preserving their structural integrity and the nitrogen content bonded to the cobalt atom. The ICP-MS analysis of the filtrate obtained after mixing ZIF-67 with the NCQD solution revealed Co atom concentrations of 0.47 wt%, 0.73 wt%, and 1.77 wt% for ZIF-67@NCQDs-0.7, ZIF-67@NCQDs-2.8, and ZIF-67@NCQDs-7.0, respectively.

XPS analysis was employed to monitor the changes in the Co–N bonding and nitrogen configurations within ZIF-67@NCQDs-x. The Co 2p spectra of ZIF-67@NCQDs-x, depicted in Fig. S3 (a), were resolved into three doublet peaks: unsaturated Co–Nx (780.2 eV), Co–N4 (781.2 eV), and a satellite peak (785.8 eV) with a 15 eV gap between the doublets [43–45]. Interestingly, the treatment of ZIF-67 with a higher NCQD concentration induced a decrease in the Co–N4 configuration accompanied by an increase in Co-Nx bonding, which resulted from the breakage of Co-N4 bonding by proton attack, whereas the pristine ZIF-67@NCQDs-0 was predominantly composed of Co–N4 bonding. In Fig. S3(b), the N1s spectra of ZIF-67@NCQDs-x were deconvoluted into five peaks at 398.1, 398.7, 399.7, 401.2, and 403.1 eV, corresponding to pyridinic N, N–Co configuration, pyrrolic N, quaternary N, and NOx, respectively. The intensities of the N–Co peaks gradually decreased, whereas that of the pyrrolic N peak significantly increased. This observation indicates that a substantial number of NCQDs, which are nitrogen-rich carbon dots primarily composed of pyrrolic N, were effectively coated on the surface of the ZIF-67@NCQDs-x particles, as shown in Fig. S1(c). Consequently, we believe that the interaction of a weakly acidic NCQD solution with ZIF-67 particles not only partially etched the particles via Co–N coordination bond cleavage, but also simultaneously coated them with N-rich carbon dots. Furthermore, considering that the etching-derived pores are homogeneously distributed and formed inside the particles without any morphological destruction, it can be concluded that the addition of 2.8 mL of NCQD solution for etching and coating represents the optimized content.

Co/NMC-x samples were prepared by the carbonization of ZIF-67@NCQDs-x. During heat treatment, Co ions within ZIF-67@NCQDs-x were thermally reduced to metallic Co nanoparticles, accompanied by the decomposition and carbonization of imidazolate ligands. This process leads to the formation of composites comprising Co nanoparticles uniformly dispersed within a nitrogen-doped carbon matrix [46]. The SEM images of Co/NMC-x shown in Fig. 2
(a)–(d) exhibited dodecahedral-shaped particles with concave edges, and some Co nanoparticles were exposed to the surface. Interestingly, the skeletons of the Co/NMC-x samples were preserved during carbonization, even though significant shrinkage of the particles occurred with an increase in the NCQD solution content for pretreating the ZIF-67 particles. This might be attributed to the decomposition of the organic ligands by heat treatment, which generated a corrosive gas containing C–N–H species [46]. However, the Co/NMC-7.0 particles were irregularly shaped with less distinct edges, possibly due to the collapse of the ZIF-67@NCQDs-7.0 structure during carbonization. The formation of the Co nanoparticles was confirmed by the XRD pattern of Co/NMC-x (Fig. 2(e)). Two peaks appeared at 44.2° and 51.5°, corresponding to the (111) and (200) planes of metallic Co, respectively. The crystallite size of Co nanoparticles, calculated based on the Debye–Scherrer equation, increased from 11.9 nm for Co/NMC-0 to 29.5 nm for Co/NMC-7.0. This growth can be attributed to the higher concentration of defective Co–Nx coordination, which is formed after the etching of ZIF-67 by the NCQD solution and subsequently readily decomposes during thermal treatment. This process allows a greater number of cobalt atoms to migrate within the composite and aggregate at elevated temperatures [28,45,47].

The influence of pre-treatment with ZIF-67 on the carbonization process was further evaluated by N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms obtained at 77 K. In Fig. 2(f), the isotherm plot of Co/NMC-0 shows type-IV with H4 hysteresis, whereas the others show type-IV with H3 hysteresis, indicating that partial etching of ZIF-67 might induce the development of mesopores during heat treatment [48]. Interestingly, the pore volumes of both the micropores and mesopores for Co/NMC-2.8, were higher than those of Co/NMC-0, resulting in an increase in the BET surface area from 254 m2/g for Co/NMC-2.8, to 341 m2/g for Co/NMC-2.8. This might be attributed to the decomposition of organic ligands during carbonization, which not only formed new defects but also enlarged the pre-existing defects in ZIF-67@NCQD-x to mesopores. The mesoporous structures in NC can provide an enlarged pathway for solvothermal sulfurization and Li-ion diffusion through the electrolyte of LIBs rather than through micropores with low wettability of the electrolyte. Wetting micropores using organic solvents is inherently challenging.

Following the thiourea-ethanol solvothermal reaction of Co/NMC-x, the Co nanoparticles could be sulfurized to CoS2 nanoparticles [49]. In Figure S4(a-h), the SEM and TEM images of CoS2/NSC-x show the maintenance of the dodecahedral structure after sulfurization and the homogenous distribution of nanoparticles within the composites. However, the CoS2 particles were noticeably larger than the Co nanoparticles in Co/NMC-x. The elemental mapping of CoS2/NSC-2.8, as shown in Figure S4 (i), confirmed that C, N, O, S, and Co were well-distributed in the composite. Notably, Co and S are observed to be co-located in the same regions. The d-spacing of nanoparticles in CoS2/NSC-x as shown in Figure S4 (j) were 0.23 and 0.28 nm, corresponding to (211) and (200) lattice fringes of crystalline CoS2, respectively, thereby confirming the formation of CoS2 nanoparticles. In Fig. 3(a), the XRD pattern of CoS2/NMC-0 shows peaks at 32.7°, 36.7°, 40.3°, 46.8°, and 55.5°, corresponding to CoS2. However, major peaks corresponding to metallic Co also appeared at 44.2° and 52.1°, indicating that partial sulfurization of the Co nanoparticles had occurred, with the dominant Co nanoparticles still present [24,25,42,50]. The metallic Co peaks of CoS2/NMC-0.7 and CoS2/NMC-2.8 gradually disappeared with increasing CoS2 peaks. Moreover, the cobalt sulfide content of CoS2/NSC-x was determined by TGA conducted in air, as shown in Figure S5. It was confirmed that the content of cobalt sulfide was increased from 14.1 % for CoS2/NMC-0 to 19.5 and 24.5 % for CoS2/NMC-0.7, and CoS2/NMC-2.8, respectively, indicating the effective conversion of Co to CoS2 during the sulfurization. This might be attributed to the abundant mesopores formed in the structure of Co/NMC-0.7 and Co/NMC-2.8, which might provide the enlarged pathway of sulfur ions through the solvent and shorten the diffusion distance inside the particles [51]. Nevertheless, broad peaks at 31.2°, 38.5°, and 55.1° appeared with the peaks of CoS2 for CoS2/NSC-7.0, which correspond to the XRD peaks of Co3S4 [52]. The formation of Co3S4 in CoS2/NSC-7.0 might be due to the formation of much larger Co nanoparticles during carbonization.

The pore structure changes during sulfurization were investigated using the N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms of CoS2/NSC-x (Fig. 3(b) and Table S2). The BET surface area was decreased to 33, 66, 76, and 59 m2/g for CoS2/NSC-0, CoS2/NSC-0.7, CoS2/NSC-2.8, and CoS2/NSC-7.0, respectively, in comparison to Co/NMC-x. The micropore volume of CoS2–NSC-x decreases significantly. Additionally, the meso/macropores of CoS2/NSC-2.8 and CoS2/NSC-7.0 decreased, whereas the volume changes of the meso/macropores for CoS2/NSC-0 and CoS2/NSC-0.7 were marginal. This might be due to the increase in particle size that occurred during the transformation from Co to CoS2 and the higher CoS2 conversion rate of CoS2/NSC-2.8, which might induce the collapse of micropores and even narrow the mesopores. Nevertheless, even after sulfurization, CoS2/NSC-2.8 maintained the highest meso-/macropore volume.

The chemical structure of CoS2/NSC-x was evaluated using XPS. The Co 2p spectra shown in Fig. 3(c) were deconvoluted into four peaks corresponding to metallic Co (778.8 eV), Co–Nx, Co (II) of CoS2, and a satellite. CoS2/NSC-0 exhibited peaks associated with metallic Co and Co (II), whereas the peak intensity of metallic Co gradually decreased as the sample was treated with a higher NCQD loading, in accordance with the XRD results. The S 2p spectra of CoS2/NSC-x were deconvoluted into doublet peaks of S2-(161.9 eV), C–S (163.6 eV), and C–S–C (164.6 eV) bonding with the gap between doublet of 1.16 eV [53,54]. The presence of S–C and C–S–C bonds implied that the thiourea-ethanol solvothermal reaction not only sulfurized metallic Co but also doped sulfur in the NC matrix. This may have originated from thiourea, which was used as the sulfur source [55,56]. The peak intensities of the S–C and C–S–C bonds for CoS2/NSC-0 were much higher than the intensity of the S2- peak, which was derived from the formation of CoS2, implying a relatively higher S-doping content on the carbon support. The peak of S2- increased in intensity for CoS2/NSC-x with higher NCQD pre-treatment. This implies that a higher amount of CoS2 was formed with a high sulfur doping content in CoS2/NSC-2.8. This observation can be attributed to the higher mesoporosity and surface area of CoS2/NSC-2.8, compared with those of CoS2/NSC-0 and CoS2/NSC-0.7. However, CoS2/NSC-7.0 showed a significant reduction in the intensity of the S–C and C–S–C peaks because severe etching during the NCQD treatment led to a considerable collapse of pores and a decrease in the surface area.

Moreover, significant changes were observed in the N 1 s spectrum of CoS2/NSC-x. The content ratios of nitrogen to carbon in CoS2/NSC-0.7, and CoS2/NSC-2.8 were 0.12, which was slightly lower than that of CoS2/NSC-0 (0.138). The decrease in nitrogen content may have resulted from the decomposition of Co–N bonds during etching with the NCQD solution. However, the coating of NCQDs on the surface of ZIF-67@NCQD-x supplied nitrogen, preventing significant nitrogen loss. In contrast, the N/C content ratio of CoS2/NSC-7.0 significantly decreased to 0.06, indicating that the loss of nitrogen derived from etching was much larger than the nitrogen addition by the NCQDs. In Fig. 3(e), the N1s spectra are deconvoluted into four peaks at 398.2, 398.7, 399.9, and 401.2 eV, which correspond to pyridinic N, N–Co, pyrrolic N, and quaternary N, respectively. The intensities of the N–Co and pyrrolic N peaks gradually decreased, whereas the intensities of the pyridinic and graphitic N increased from CoS2/NSC-0 to CoS2/NSC-7.0. This suggests that the NCQDs were well-coated on the surface of ZIF-67 and acted as a source of graphitic and pyridinic nitrogen. This phenomenon occurred because the as-prepared NCQDs predominantly possessed pyrrolic N, which was subsequently converted into pyridinic and graphitic N during carbonization (Fig. S1(c) and (d)). These alterations are expected to enhance the stability of LIBs because graphitic- and pyridinic N have been known to be more favored by lithium polysulfides than pyrrolic N, thus preventing sulfur loss [57,58].

The electrochemical performance of CoS2/NSC-x as an anode material in LIBs was also investigated. The CV curve shown in Fig. 4 (a) exhibited reductive peaks at 1.50 and 1.23 V, which originated from the Li-ion intercalation to form LixCoS2 and the subsequent lithiation converting LixCoS2 to Co0 and Li2S, respectively [6]. An additional reductive peak at 0.8 V was observed in the first cycle but disappeared in the subsequent cycles. This peak was attributed to the formation of an irreversible solid electrolyte interface (SEI) formation [59,60]. In oxidation, two peaks at 2.02 and 2.43 V were associated with the delithiation of Li2S and the oxidation of metallic Co to form CoS2 [59]. For the subsequent cycles, all peaks except the reductive peaks at 0.8 V consistently reappeared, indicating that the lithiation/delithiation reaction of CoS2 was reproducible.

The rate capability was tested with the various current densities from 0.1 to 2.0 A g−1. In the voltage profile of CoS2/NSC-2.8 shown in Fig. 4(b), the two reduction plateaus were observed at 1.8 and 1.3 V for discharge, whereas two oxidation plateaus appeared at 2.0 and 2.4 V during charge. These voltage plateaus agree well with the peaks observed in the CV data. The capacities at the current density of 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0, and 2.0 A g−1 were 664, 612, 540, 465, and 392 mAh g−1, respectively. The initial capacities of CoS2/NSC-0, CoS2/NSC-0.7, and CoS2/NSC-7.0 were 567, 571, and 450, respectively. Additionally, the capacity retention rates of 2.0 A g−1 were 40, 45, and 47 % for CoS2/NSC-0, CoS2/NSC-0.7, and CoS2/NSC-7.0, respectively, implied that the CoS2/NSC-2.8 exhibited the highest capacity with the enhanced C-rate capability. These findings suggest that the pre-treatment of ZIF-67 with a higher NCQD loading improved its electrochemical performance. This enhancement can be attributed to the increased conversion rate from metallic Co to CoS2 during sulfurization, as well as the increase in mesopores within the structures, which facilitated the diffusion of Li ions through the electrolyte.

The enhanced C-rate capability of CoS2/NSC-x was further studied using EIS measurements. The Nyquist plots of CoS2/NSC-x were fitted using electrical circuit models, as shown in the inset of Fig. 4 (d). In these plots, the semicircles represent the charge transfer resistance (Rct) at the interface between the electrode and the electrolyte, whereas the straight line appearing in the low-frequency region represents the Warburg impedance (ZW), which is related to Li-ion diffusion [61]. The value of Rct was 77 Ω for CoS2/NSC-0, and it decreased to 57 Ω and 55 Ω for CoS2/NSC-0.7, and CoS2/NSC-2.8, respectively. Moreover, the slope of the plot in the Warburg region was steeper for CoS2/NSC-2.8, than for CoS2/NSC-0.7 and CoS2/NSC-0, which indicates that the diffusion resistance decreased. Thus, it was confirmed that the use of 2.8 ml of NCQD in the pretreatment of ZIF-67 led to improved Li-ion diffusion and enhanced electronic conductivity, ultimately realizing fast charge/discharge. However, the Rct of CoS2/NSC-7.0 was significantly higher than that of CoS2/NSC-2.8, primarily because of the larger particle size of cobalt sulfides and the lower heteroatom content in the carbon matrix.

The cycling stabilities of CoS2/NSC-x were evaluated at a current density of 0.2 A g−1 And 2.0 A g−1. As shown in Fig. 4(e), the initial capacity of CoS2/NSC-2.8, 621 mAh g−1, was the highest among those of CoS2/NSC-x. After 100 cycles, it retained a capacity of 475 mAh g−1, which corresponds to 77 % of its initial capacity. In contrast, CoS2/NSC-0 exhibits a capacity retention of 71 %. Interestingly, CoS2/NSC-0.7 exhibited a capacity of 416 mAh g−1 after 100 cycles, demonstrating the most stable cycling performance with a capacity retention of 83 %. This indicates that the ZIF-67 even with slight etching and coating with 0.7 mL NCQD solution enhanced the cycling stability, even though the capacity increase was marginal compared to that of CoS2/NSC-0, confirming the etching and coating effect of the NCQD solution on ZIF-67. In higher current density of 2.0 A g−1 as shown in Figure S7, CoS2/NSC-2.8 exhibited the highest capacity during cycling compared to rest of CoS2/NSC-x electrodes. The capacity of CoS2/NSC-2.8 maintained to 162 mAh g−1 after 200 cycles, which proves again the stable cyclability of CoS2/NSC-2.8 in fast charge/discharge.

The kinetics of Li-ion storage reaction in CoS2/NSC-x was investigated by conducting cyclic voltammetry with various scan rates from 0.1 to 1.0 mV s−1 as shown in Fig. 5 (a) and Figure S6. The b-value enabled us to distinguish the contribution of diffusion- or capacitive-controlled reactions at a designated potential in the CV. The b values were calculated using the following equation [62,63]:
(1)

where i and v are the peak current and scan rate, respectively. where a and b are constants; the b value corresponds to the slope of the graph plotted between log i and log v (equation (2)). The b value is typically determined between 0.5 and 1.0 depending on the electrochemical behavior of the materials whether the reaction is diffusion-dominated or capacitive-controlled. In Fig. 5(b), the b values of all CoS2/NSC-x samples range between 0.65 and 0.8, indicating that the lithiation/delithiation of CoS2/NSC-x is a pseudocapacitive reaction. CoS2/NSC-2.8 exhibited higher b values for all four peaks, as indicated in Fig. 5(a), than CoS2/NSC-x.

Furthermore, considering that the peak current at a given voltage is composed of the charge derived from the capacitive-controlled reaction (k1 v) and the diffusion-controlled reaction (k2 v 1/2), the contribution of each behavior can be quantified using equation (3) [64]:

The k1 and k2 values were obtained from plots of i(V)/v1/2 versus v1/2. In Fig. 5 (c) and Figure S6 (d-f), the capacitive contribution of CoS2/NSC-x was calculated from the CV curve at a scan rate of 0.2 mV s−1. It is notable, that the capacitive contribution increased from 41 % for CoS2/NSC-0 to 52 % for CoS2/NSC-2.8, as the higher NCQD content increased during pre-treatment. As the scan rate of CV increased from 0.1 to 1.0 mV s−1 as shown in Fig. 5(d), the capacitive contribution of CoS2/NSC-2.8 increased from 46 % to 73 % whereas that of CoS2/NSC-0 increased from 36 % to 63 %. Thus, the higher capacitive contribution of CoS2/NSC-2.8 might result in the enhancement of the rate performance, which might be attributed to the development of porous structures with a larger surface area and meso/macropore volume, and a higher content of heteroatom doping in the carbon matrix.

The degradation behavior of CoS2/NSC-x based electrodes was further investigated through post-mortem analysis. As shown in Figure S8, CoS2/NSC-0 and CoS2/NSC-2.8 particles in the electrode were altered their morphologies after 200 cycles, which might be resulted from the volume changes occurred during cycling. However, CoS2/NSC-2.8 particles were obviously observed, while particles in the CoS2/NSC-0 electrode were covered by thick layers after 200 cycles. These layers might be originated from the formation of thick SEI layers and Li2S, which reacted irreversibly during charging [13,65,66]. Moreover, as shown in insets in the Figure S8, a separator of a cell with CoS2/NSC-0 after 200 cycles showed yellow-colored, which might indicate the dissolution of lithium polysulfides, while a separator in a cell of CoS2/NSC-2.8 exhibited no color change. It has been reported that the formation of lithium polysulfide during cycling is one of the main reasons of electrode degradation for CoS2-based electrodes [6,60,67,68]. The continuous cycling can result in incomplete oxidation of metallic Co, leading to the accumulation of metallic Co after charging. The shortage of Co oxidation hinders the conversion of Li2S to CoS2, which might induce further oxidation to form liquid phase high-order lithium polysulfides. Ultimately, the loss of active materials leads to a decrease in capacity during cycling. However, no yellowish lithium polysulfide dissolved out for CoS2/NSC-2.8, indicating that lithium polysulfides were effectively captured and metallic Co were reversibly converted with the Li2S into CoS2 during long-term cycling.

We introduced a unique strategy to control the porosity and heteroatom doping of ZIF-67-derived CoS2/nitrogen- and sulfur-co-doped carbon composites to enhance the stability of LIBs. Our approach involves the use of an NCQD solution obtained through rigorous oxidation of NMP. In addition, ZIF-67 was treated with varying amounts of the weakly acidic NCQD solution before carbonization. Through this process, we observed that the NCQD pre-treatment partially dissociated the Co–N bonds and simultaneously coated the surface of ZIF-67 with NCQDs, which acted as a nitrogen source. This pre-treatment leads to increased mesopore porosity during carbonization, facilitating the transformation of metallic Co to CoS2 during solvothermal sulfurization. Additionally, the carbonization of the NCQD coating layer introduces pyridinic N and graphitic N, whereas thiourea-ethanol sulfurization incorporates sulfur doping into the nitrogen-doped carbon network. This sulfur doping enhanced the ability of the material to attract lithium polysulfides, contributing to improved battery performance. By applying the optimized 2.8 mL NCQD solution for ZIF-67 pre-treatment, the resulting CoS2/NSC-2.8, used as an anode material, achieved a higher capacity of 664 mAh g−1, enhanced C-rate capability, and improved cycling stability compared to pristine CoS2/NSC.
